coredata.dc:title: Upconversion nanocomposite CeO2:Tm3+/attapulgite intermediated by carbon quantum dots for photocatalytic desulfurization
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiazhang
Ma, Sujuan
Qian, Heming
Zhang, Yuying
Zuo, Shixiang
Yao, Chao
coredata.dc:description: Upconversion CeO2:Tm3+/attapulgite (ATP) nanocomposites intermediated by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a facile deposition method using hexamethylenetetramine as precipitator. The products were characterized by various techniques. The photocatalytic degradation of dibenzothiophene (DBT) in simulated gasoline was conducted under visible light irradiation by taking advantage of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites as catalyst. The influence of Tm3+ doping amount and the mass fraction of CeO2 to ATP on the desulfurization performance were investigated. Results indicated that the CeO2:Tm3+ nanoparticles were coupled uniformly on the CQDs/ATP surface. The desulfurization rate of DBT reached the highest 96% when the molar fraction of Tm3+ in CeO2 was 2% and the mass fraction of CeO2 to ATP was 20 wt%. The enhanced photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization was attributed to the upconversion property of CeO2:Tm3+ which transformed visible light into ultraviolet light increasing the utilization of solar energy, while CeO2:Tm3+ built indirect Z-scheme heterojunction with modified ATP mediated by CQDs, both favored the separation of the charges carriers and benefits the redox potentials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CeO2
Carbon quantum dots
Attapulgite
Upconversion
Photocatalysis
Desulfurization

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of ATP, CQDs/ATP, CeO2, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of ATP, CQDs/ATP, CeO2, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (b) the estimated band gap of ATP and CeO2:Tm3+.
FT-IR spectra of ATP, CQDs/ATP, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP.
TEM images of (a) ATP, (b) CQDs/ATP, (c) CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (d) HRTEM of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP.

XPS spectra of ATP, CQDs/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s of CQDs/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (c) Si 2p of ATP, CQDs/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (d) Ce 3d of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (e) Tm 4d of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, (f) VB-XPS of ATP.

(a) upconversion spectra of various Tm doped CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP under the excitation at 488 nm laser, (b) photoluminescence spectra of various loading amounts CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP under the excitation at 300 nm laser.
Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy of ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP composites with different loadings.
Photocatalytic desulfurization of DBT under visible light irradiation with various contents of CeO2:Tm3+ to ATP and H2O2 as blank.
Indirect Z-scheme photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization mechanism for CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP.

head.ce:title: Upconversion nanocomposite CeO2:Tm3+/attapulgite intermediated by carbon quantum dots for photocatalytic desulfurization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Delaware, Newark, DE 19716, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center, Changzhou University, Changzhou 213164, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Catalysis and Green Manufacturing Collaborative Innovation Center

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Upconversion CeO2:Tm3+/attapulgite (ATP) nanocomposites intermediated by carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by a facile deposition method using hexamethylenetetramine as precipitator. The products were characterized by various techniques. The photocatalytic degradation of dibenzothiophene (DBT) in simulated gasoline was conducted under visible light irradiation by taking advantage of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites as catalyst. The influence of Tm3+ doping amount and the mass fraction of CeO2 to ATP on the desulfurization performance were investigated. Results indicated that the CeO2:Tm3+ nanoparticles were coupled uniformly on the CQDs/ATP surface. The desulfurization rate of DBT reached the highest 96% when the molar fraction of Tm3+ in CeO2 was 2% and the mass fraction of CeO2 to ATP was 20 wt%. The enhanced photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization was attributed to the upconversion property of CeO2:Tm3+ which transformed visible light into ultraviolet light increasing the utilization of solar energy, while CeO2:Tm3+ built indirect Z-scheme heterojunction with modified ATP mediated by CQDs, both favored the separation of the charges carriers and benefits the redox potentials.

ce:section.ce:para: Sulfur oxides (SOx) are widely recognized as one of the primary air pollutants causing acid rain and haze which are harmful for the eco-system. In order to reduce the harmful impact, there is an urgent need to reduce the sulfur content in the fuel [1–3]. Conventional hydrodesulfurization has been widely used in industrialization, however it requires high temperature, extreme pressure and large amount of hydrogen consumption [4,5]. In addition, dibenzothiophene and its derivatives are very stable under hydrogenation conditions, making it difficult to achieve deep desulfurization. Up to date, adsorption desulfurization [6], extractive desulfurization [7], biological desulfurization [8] and oxidative desulfurization [9,10] have been widely employed to remove sulfur-containing materials from oils. Among them, photocatalysis assisted oxidation desulfurization has attracted much attention because of its low cost, facile operation, no consumption of hydrogen and high desulfurization efficiency. For instance, Hitam et al. [11] obtained Cu doped amorphous TiO2 catalysts for visible-light-induced oxidative desulfurization for dibenzothiophene. Rezvani et al. [12] prepared PMoCu@MgCu2O4-PVA as a high-performance heterogeneous nanocatalyst for oxidative desulfurization of gasoline. However, many photocatalysts require ultraviolet light radiation or noble metals making them rather difficult into practical application. Therefore, it is important to develop cost-effective photocatalysis materials in attempt to utilize solar energy to remove sulfur species from petroleum oil more efficiently.

As a clay mineral material with ideal chemical formula Mg5Si8O20(OH)2(OH2)4·4H2O, ATP has been widely used to immobilize semiconductors in order to solve the above problems and reduce the cost of the catalyst. ATP has unique 1-D layer-chain structure [13,14], and possesses outstanding adsorption capacity due to its abundant internal channels [15,16]. Furthermore, its large specific surface area makes it as a good candidate to form functionalized nanocomposites [17,18]. In our previous work, CeO2/ATP/g-C3N4 ternary nanocomposite was obtained for photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization, and the degradation rate of DBT can reach up to 98% [19]. Interestingly, recent work found that ATP contains transition metal ionic especially Fe substitution in the structure, rendering its UV responsive property to some extent [20].

Up to date, how to improve the utilization of solar light has remained as a challenge. Intriguingly, upconversion (UC) materials have drawn numerous considerations such as RE3+-ions (e.g., Er3+, Tm3+ and Ho3+) doping in oxides, fluorides or phosphates [21–26]. In general, UC emission efficiency is strongly dependent on the host lattice and its interaction with the dopant ions. Therefore, low phonon frequency host materials can minimize the multi-phonon relaxation rate and offer high UC efficiency [27–29]. Cerium oxide (CeO2) is considered as one of the most reactive rare earth metal oxides, possessing excellent thermal stability, chemical stability and low phonon cutoff energy, making it suitable for decreasing the possibility of nonradiative transitions which result in a high quantum yield of UC process [30–32]. However, there are limited reports on the UC emission of lanthanum-doped CeO2 at present [33–37], not to mention the construction of UC CeO2 based heterojunctions. What's more, the typical type-II heterojunction has the disadvantage with respect to the weakened reduction and oxidation potential, leading to the decrease of photocatalytic capability. CQDs have been reported as a mediator to accept and transfer photogenerated electrons, which avoid the combination of photogenerated charges. CQDs are able to improve the photocatalytic performance through building the heterojunction system due to its high absorption coefficient and rapid electron transportation properties [38–40]. Pan et al. found that the CQDs can be use as excellent electron mediator between CdS and BiOCl [41]. Therefore, building indirect Z-scheme heterojunction with modified ATP and CeO2:Tm3+ intermediated by CQDs is promising.

In this work, the modified ATP was coupled with CQDs first and then loaded by Tm3+ doped CeO2 for the sake of utilization of solar light, meanwhile an indirect Z-scheme heterostructure was built mediated by CQDs which is expected to facilitate the charge transfer and preserve high reduction-oxidation potential. The effects of photocatalytic desulfurization efficiency were investigated by adjusting the doping amount of Tm3+ into CeO2 and the loading amount of CeO2:Tm3+ on the modified ATP. This work is anticipated to provide a strategy to develop efficient photocatalyst with good stability and excellent activity for deep desulfurization.

Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3·6H2O), thulium nitrate hexahydrate (Tm(NO3)3·6H2O), and Hexamethylenetetramine (C6H12N4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., China. Attapulgite was provided by Nanjing University Zijin Co. Ltd., Jiangsu, China. All reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification.

The CQDs/ATP nanocomposites were obtained by our previous work [42]. CQDs/ATP with 1 g of raw ATP was dispersed in 100 mL deionized water, followed by adding Ce(NO3)3·6H2O and Tm(NO3)3·6H2O with magnetic stirring. Hexamethylenetetramine (molar ratio 1:5) was then added to the solution. The mixture was heated in a water bath of 80 °C for 2 h. The precipitate was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and absolute ethanol for three times. The resulting solid was dried in vacuum at 80 °C for 12 h, and subsequently calcined at 300 °C in air for 3 h.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was obtained through a Rigaku D/max 2500 PC diffractometer, using Cu Kɑ radiation (λ = 0.154056 nm) in 2θ values of 2–80° at room temperature. The ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of nanocomposites were determined on a UV–Vis spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet Evolution 500) equipped with an integrating sphere. FT-IR spectra of catalysts were obtained using a Nicolet 460 with diffuse reflectance sampling accessory at a resolution of 4 cm−1. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements of samples were carried out using a JEOL-2100 coupled with a Gatan 832 CCD operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The chemical states of elements in the samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Quantum 2000 S canning ESCA Microprobe instrument using Al Kɑ, and the C 1s signal was set to a position of 284.6 eV. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements at room temperature were collected on a LS45 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 450 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiment was carried out on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation.

Photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization was performed by degrading the simulated gasoline with 200 ppm which was prepared through dissolving dibenzothiophene (DBT) into n-octane. The experiment was carried out in an GHX-2 instrument which contained a 300 W xenon lamp work as the light source with a filter to cut-off all the ultraviolet light lower than 420 nm to ensure that only visible light is present. Under the atmospheric pressure and room temperature, the catalysts and simulated gasoline (mass ratio 1:500) were added into the reaction apparatus. Meanwhile 30 wt% H2O2 as an oxidant was added into the reaction system. Before the irradiation, the mixture solution was dispersed in the dark for 30 min to establish adsorption–desorption balance. The samples were collected every 30 min, and the supernatant was extracted by adding acetonitrile, finally the content of sulfur was detected by an ultraviolet fluorescence sulfur measuring apparatus (THA2000S). The desulfurization efficiency was obtained by the desulfurization rate D (%):
D =
1 −
C t
/
C 0
× 100 %
Where C0 was the initial sulfur content in the solution at t = −30 min, Ct was the sulfur content in the solution at time t after catalytic reaction.

The powder XRD patterns of ATP, CQDs/ATP, CeO2, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP are shown in Fig. 1 . The ATP and CQDs/ATP reveal identical pattern of three main peaks at 8.5°, 19.6° and 27.3°, corresponding to (110), (0 4 0) and (4 0 0) planes of ATP. Characteristic diffraction peaks of pure CeO2 locate at 28.5°, 33.1°, 47.4° and 56.3°, which are assigned to (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes. When the Tm3+ is doped into CeO2, the intensity of the peaks is weakened while shifting slightly to higher Bragg angle since the radius of Tm3+ (88 pm) is smaller than Ce3+ (102 pm), suggesting that part of Tm3+ may be incorporated into the CeO2 lattice leading to the decrease of the interplanar spacing. Meanwhile with the loading of CeO2:Tm3+, the intensity of CeO2 characteristic peaks rises whereas the CQDs/ATP characteristic peaks decrease, implying that a large number of CeO2:Tm3+ nanoparticles are interconnected with CQDs/ATP. It should be noted that the intensity of the (110) peak at 8.5° for ATP in the CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites is weaker than that of ATP, and the peak shift slightly to higher Bragg angle, which may be attributable to the decrease of the interplanar spacing of (1 1 0) basal plane of ATP. A plausible rational for the basal spacing shrinkage is associated with the partial incorporation of CeO2:Tm3+ nanoparticles into the internal channels of ATP leading to well-defined immobilization of CeO2:Tm3+ on ATP.

The UV–Vis spectra of ATP, CQDs/ATP, CeO2, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP are shown in Fig. 2(a). The absorption spectrum of ATP demonstrates that it is responsive in the ultraviolet light region, whereas the absorption peak of CQDs/ATP is apparently red-shift and the absorbance intensity is higher compared to ATP, which is consistent with our previous study [42]. On the other hand, the absorption peak of CeO2:Tm3+ is red-shifted compared to CeO2 owing to the Tm3+ doping, suggesting that the introduction of Tm3+ may shorten the band gap of CeO2:Tm3+. It is worth mentioning that the absorption peak of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP is red-shifted compared to CQDs/ATP attributed to the loading of CeO2:Tm3+, suggesting that the introduction of CeO2:Tm3+ enhance the absorption of visible light. The absorbance intensity of the CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites in the visible and ultraviolet light range are both higher than CQDs/ATP due to the formation of heterojunction structure, leading to the improved UV–Vis response capability. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the estimated band gap (Eg) of ATP and CeO2:Tm3+ is 3.16 eV and 2.64 eV, respectively.

The FT-IR spectra of ATP, CQDs/ATP, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP are shown in Fig. 3 . As far as the ATP is concerned, the band at 3541 cm−1 is assigned to Al–OH stretching vibration [43]. As for CQDs/ATP, the band at 1657 cm−1 is assigned to –COO– stretching vibration for the esterification reaction between the hydroxyl radical of ATP and the carboxy of CQDs，which has been revealed in our previous report [42]. The weak band located at about 479 cm−1 may be assigned to the CeO stretching vibration of CeO2, which is further approved by the Raman spectrum shown in Fig. S1. High-intensity phonon vibration appears around 462 cm−1 is attributed to the symmetric F2g mode for cubic CeO2. Comparing the FT-IR spectra of CQDs/ATP, CeO2:Tm3+ and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, a new peak from CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP is identified near 547 cm−1, which may be associated with the absorption peak of Ce–O–C bond suggesting the formation of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs heterostructure.

The TEM morphology of the ATP, CQDs/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 4 . It can be found that the ATP owns 1-D fibrous structure with uniform diameters ranging from 30 to 50 nm in Fig. 4(a), and the CQDs are uniformly coated on the surface of ATP in Fig. 4(b). As shown in Fig. 4(c), CeO2:Tm3+ and CQDs are uniformly coated on the surface of ATP. Notably, clear lattice fringes can be seen corresponding to the (101) plane of CQDs and (111) plane of CeO2 in Fig. 4(d), while CeO2 and CQDs nanoparticles form coherent junctions on the ATP surface leading to efficient electron transfer and improved charge separation between the two components.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis is employed to investigate the valence state and surface chemical compositions of the products. Fig. 5 exhibits the survey scan of ATP, CQDs/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposite. Ce, Tm, O, Si, Al, and C peaks are observed in Fig. 5(a) with the absence of any other elements. The C 1s spectra of CQDs/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP are shown in Fig. 5(b) respectively. Four peaks of CQDs/ATP can be identified, specifically, 284.6 eV is attributed to the CC bond with a sp2 orbital, 286.4 eV is assigned to CO, 287.9 eV is assigned to CO bond originating from the CQDs, and 289.5 eV is assigned to CO bonds originating from the esterification reaction between the carboxy group of CQDs and the hydroxyl group of ATP. As for CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, corresponding peaks showing an obvious shift to lower binding energy compared to CQDs/ATP, indicative of the formation of Ce–O–C bond, which is consistent with the results of FT-IR. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the binding energy of Si 2p core level is 102.8 eV for CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, showing a slight shift to lower binding energy compared to ATP and CQDs/ATP which are at 102.9 eV and 103.1 eV, respectively, indicating the formation of C–O–Si bond. In our previous work, the CQDs were immobilized on the surface of ATP through the Si–O–C bonding [42], however in the present work, the electronegativity of Ce atoms is less than that of Si atoms, suggesting that the Si atoms may be substituted by more polarizable Ce atoms in the CeO2:Tm3+ on ATP matrix. Therefore, it is assumed that part of C-O-Si bond for the CQDs/ATP is replaced by partial C-O-Ce bond for CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP. The Fig. 5(d) shows Ce 3d XPS peaks, where m and n indicate the spin-orbit coupling 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 respectively. The peaks of m, n, m 2, n 2, m 3, n 3 are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Ce4+, while the peaks of m 1 and n 1 are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Ce3+, suggesting that a mixture of Ce3+/Ce4+ oxidation states exist on the surface of the composite [44]. In Fig. 5(e), the binding energy of Tm 4d core level is 176.1 eV for CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, showing the existence of Tm3+ in the composite. Fig. 5(f) shows the VB-XPS pattern of ATP, suggesting that the value of VB for ATP is determined to be 3.20 eV.

Fig. 6 shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP which reveals the influence of Tm3+ doping on PL emission. Fig. 6(a) indicates the upconversion PL spectrum of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP. The intensity of emission ultraviolet light is the strongest when the amount of Tm3+ doping is 2% illuminated by the light of 488 nm. As the content of Tm3+ further increases, concentration quenching may occur for CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP, and the intensity of emission decreases. This phenomenon is attributed to the energy migration and cross-relaxation energy loss to the surface quenchers. Fig. 6(b) shows the photoluminescence spectrum with various loading amounts illuminated by the light of 300 nm. It is well known that the higher intensity suggests higher recombination rate in the PL spectrums. When the mass fraction of CeO2:Tm3+/ATP is 20 wt%, the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole is higher than the others, where the CQDs acting as a trap for the combination of electron-holes between ATP and CeO2:Tm3+, leading to the separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes. Conversely, the separation efficiency of electron-holes decreases when the mass ratio of CeO2:Tm3+ to ATP is more than 20 wt%. It can be rationalized that excess CeO2:Tm3+ may provide more electrons destroying the balance of the recombination for electron-hole pairs.

Fig. 7 shows the impedance plots of ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites. The test result shows the semicircle structure, while the electrical performance of the electrode can be analyzed with the Nyquist Equivalent circuit analysis. The relative sizes of circular arc radius correspond to the charge transfer resistance and separation efficiency of electron-hole in Nyquist plots [45]. As shown in Fig. 7, the Nyquist plot shows that when the mass fraction of CeO2:Tm3+/ATP is 20 wt%, the diameter of the semicircle is much smaller than that of the others, implying the better separation efficiency of electron-hole and the faster transmission rate [46], which is consistent with the PL result.

The photocatalytic oxidation desulfurization performance of ATP, CeO2:Tm3+/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP with various loading amounts is measured under visible light irradiation. Fig. 8 shows the comparison for H2O2, ATP, CeO2:Tm3+/ATP and CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP. The results show that the removal rate of DBT in model gasoline gradually increases as the reaction proceeds and reaches a balance. The desulfurization rate of H2O2 is only about 8.2% after reaction for 3 h, and the CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposite achieve the highest 96% when the mass fraction of CeO2:Tm3+ to ATP is 20 wt%, due to that the CeO2:Tm3+ may convert visible light to ultraviolet light to stimulate ATP, meanwhile CeO2:Tm3+ is self-excited by visible light, then the photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of ATP are transferred to the valence band of CeO2:Tm3+ by the CQDs which separate the charges efficiently, thereby increasing the utilization of visible light. After desulfurization reaction, the 20 wt% catalyst was separated by filtration to remove residual oil and H2O2, and dried in oven to obtain the recycled catalyst. The reusability of recycled catalyst was then performed. After three recycle times, the desulfurization rate can maintain as high as 95%, indicative of remarkable reusability. The enhanced desulfurization activity and recyclability of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP is ascribed to the ATP skeleton owing outstanding mechanical and thermal stability [19]. However, excess CeO2:Tm3+ may provide more electrons recombined with holes resulting in the decrease of the separation efficiency, leading to the gradual decline of the desulfurization rate.

Fig. 9 shows the formation process of CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites. Based on the pre-prepared CQDs/ATP, Tm3+ doped CeO2 nanoparticles were grown on the CQDs/ATP surface via homogeneous precipitation with hexamethylenetetramineas as precipitator. The band edge position of Ce0.98Tm0.02O2 in this study is estimated according to the following formulas [47].
(1)
E VB
= χ −
E e
+ 0 .
5E g
(2)
E CB
=
E VB
−
E g

Where EVB represents the valence band (VB) edge potential, ECB represents the conduction band (CB) edge potential, Eg represents the band gap of the semiconductor, Ee represents the energy of free electrons of the hydrogen (~4.50 eV) and χ represents the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor. The CB and VB of modified ATP are determined to be 0.04 eV and 3.20 eV according to the aformentioned UV–Vis spectrum and VB-XPS spectrum, respectively. The CB and VB of CeO2:Tm3+ are calculated to be to −0.21 eV and 2.43 eV, respectively. Therefore, the VB edge potential of modifed ATP is more positive than that of CeO2:Tm3+, while the CB edge potential of CeO2:Tm3+ is more negative than that of modified ATP. According to the above analysis, the mechanism of photocatalytic oxidation desulfurization is put forward. As shown in Fig. 10 , the 3H6 state of Tm3+ is converted to the 1G4 state under the visible light irradiation though ground state absorption process(step Ӏ) [48], in which part of Tm3+ at the 1G4 state will be non-radiated to the 3H4 state by a multi-phonon relaxation process, and the 3H4 state can be converted to the 1I6 state by excited state absorption process, finally the 283 nm ultraviolet light can be obtained corresponding to the emission of 1I6 state to 3H6 state. Modified ATP is excited by ultraviolet light (step II) to generate electron-hole pairs, where electrons in the conduction band of ATP are transferred to the valence band of CeO2:Tm3+ through CQDs. Therefore, the electrons in CB of CeO2:Tm3+ with high reduction potential are preserved to give rise to hydroxyl radicals (·OH) with H2O2 in the solution, the holes in VB of modified ATP with high oxidation potential are maintained to generate ·OH with the hydroxyl ion (OH−). In consequence, CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP ternary nanocomposite performs enhanced desulfurization performance by establishing an indirect Z-scheme structure.

In summary, CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP ternary photocatalysts with various doping ratios and loading amounts were successfully prepared. Adequate Tm3+ dopant increased the utilization of visible-light due to the upconversion ability of CeO2:Tm3+, and CQDs functioned as electron mediator which effectively transferred electrons to construct indirect Z-scheme structure preserving high reduction-oxidation potential between modified ATP and CeO2:Tm3+. The obtained CeO2:Tm3+/CQDs/ATP nanocomposites performed remarkable photocatalytic activity for the desulfurization of simulated gas oil. The photocatalytic degradation rate of DBT can reach the highest 96% within 3 h with remarkable reusability when the Tm3+ doping amount in CeO2 is 2 mol% and the mass fraction of CeO2:Tm3+ to ATP is 20 wt%. This cost-effective upconversion plus Z-scheme heterostructure may provide potential alternatives for photocatalysis applications in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51674043, 51702026), Jiangsu Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalytic Materials and Technology (BM2012110) and Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (SJCX18_0951).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.powtec.2019.04.004.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave hydrothermal synthesis of BiP1−xVxO4/attapulgite nanocomposite with efficient photocatalytic performance for deep desulfurization

Extractive desulfurization and denitrogenation of fuels using functional acidic ionic liquids
Methoxylation pathway in biodesulfurization of model organosulfur compounds with Mycobacterium sp
Graphene-analogue hexagonal BN supported with tungsten-based ionic liquid for oxidative desulfurization of fuels
Synthesis of Ag-AgBr/Al-MCM-41 nanocomposite and its application in photocatalytic oxidative desulfurization of dibenzothiophene
Effect of carbon-interaction on structure–photoactivity of Cu doped amorphous TiO2 catalysts for visible-light-oriented oxidative desulphurization of dibenzothiophene
